title: Sisal vs Carpet Scratching Posts — Which Is Better for Your Cat?
seoTitle: Sisal vs Carpet Scratching Posts – Honest Comparison (2026)
metaDescription: Sisal or carpet? Sisal lasts 5x longer and cats prefer it 3:1. See the full comparison on durability, cost, and cat preference before you buy.
author: GetPawsy Pet Care Team

content.text: In durability tests and cat preference studies, sisal rope outperforms carpet 3:1. Cats prefer the resistance and texture of sisal for claw maintenance. Carpet posts shred quickly, leaving loose fibers that cats ingest. Sisal lasts 3–5 years vs 6–12 months for carpet. The only advantage of carpet is initial softness — which isn't what cats actually want from a scratching surface.

Scratching serves three purposes: claw sheath removal, scent marking, and muscle stretching. Sisal provides the firm resistance needed for effective claw maintenance — like a nail file vs a pillow. Cats instinctively seek rough, resistant textures. This is why outdoor cats prefer tree bark over grass.

There are two types: sisal rope (wound around posts) and sisal fabric (flat woven sheets). Rope is more common and preferred by most cats for the vertical groove texture. Fabric is smoother and better for horizontal scratching surfaces. The best cat trees use rope on vertical posts and fabric on angled ramps.

Carpet is fine for platform surfaces where cats sit or sleep. Some trees use carpet on ramps for traction. But load-bearing scratching posts should always be sisal. If your tree has carpet-wrapped posts, expect to replace them within a year with an active scratcher.

Choose cat trees with sisal-wrapped posts for durability and cat satisfaction. Our [cat trees for large cats](/cat/cat-trees-for-large-cats) all feature thick sisal posts rated for heavy use. For more on choosing the right tree, see our [cat tree stability guide](/guides/cat-tree-stability-guide).

faq.question: Is sisal or carpet better for cats?
faq.answer: Sisal is better. Cats prefer the texture 3:1 over carpet, it lasts 3–5x longer, and it provides the resistance needed for proper claw maintenance. Carpet shreds quickly and can cause fiber ingestion.

faq.question: How long does sisal rope last on a cat tree?
faq.answer: High-quality sisal rope (8mm+ diameter) lasts 3–5 years with daily use. Thin sisal (under 6mm) may fray within 12 months. Look for trees advertising thick sisal wrapping for longevity.

faq.question: Can I re-wrap sisal on my cat tree?
faq.answer: Yes. Buy 8mm natural sisal rope and wrap it tightly around the post using hot glue at the start and end points. Re-wrapping costs about $10–15 per post and takes 15 minutes.
